title: Work package 2. Argentina.
text:
Argentina has a population of around 45 million people living in 23 provinces and the autonomous city of Buenos Aires. The province of La Pampa has 361,859 inhabitants living in an area of 143,440 km² (2022 Census). The public health system in the province of La Pampa consists of 126 healthcare facilities located across 80 localities, with about 11,000 authorized users, including health professionals and administrative staff. This health care network is administratively organized into five health zones.



Argentina map, its provinces, and health districts in La Pampa province


The Health Information System (SIS, from its acronym in Spanish) provides an online appointment system and a central appointment desk for referral to secondary and tertiary levels of care. This information system is crucial for data access and evaluating the impact of health policies. However, the full capacity of the SIS for the diabetes programme management and decision making has not been exploited yet. Local challenges include the lack of the technical skills to perform the data linkage and analyzing the data.
According to the 4th National Survey of Risk Factors (INDEC, 2019), the prevalence of diabetes in La Pampa province was estimated at 12.7% in 2019. Approximately 17,000 patients are receiving diabetes care within the public health system, with 4,300 of these being insulin-treated (Provincial Healthcare IT System). “La Pampa Diabetes Programme” was established in the same year under the management of the Evita General Community Hospital with the objectives to improve access to care, ensure timely referrals and appropriate treatment, and implement a comprehensive approach to reduce diabetes-related complications and enhance the quality of life for patients.

title: Work package 1. Project management, collaboration and technical documentation
text:
This WP has three objectives, 1) to lead the overall management of the project, 2) to coordinate the collaboration between WPs and 3) ensure the transfer of technical skills for the implementation of the open-source software, and the implementation of a high-quality technical documentation of the project following open science and reproducibility principles.
WP1 will implement the Seedcase Project, an open-source data infrastructure tool that is compliant with the FAIR principles (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable). It is designed to support modern, scalable, and efficient approaches to health information systems [REF-seedcase webpage]. By using state-of-the-art software engineering practices, Seedcase project ensures that health data are FAIR, meeting a robust stewardship, open and reproducible science. This will enable the integration of high quality, standardized registry data from Argentina, Mexico and Colombia into a shared framework. By documenting the full data life cycle, from generation to analysis and interpretation, Seedcase will deliver transparent, reproducible and policy-relevant data resources for research, health system planning, and policy making within diabetes health care [REF Seedcase].

WP1 will deploy the technical infrastructure and build local technical capacity to maintain and expand these systems, prioritizing sustainability (maintainability over time), capacity building and equity in digital health. The emphasis on co-design and open documentation ensures that the implementation of Seedcase is context-specific, scalable, and potentially transferable to other non-communicable diseases. This approach strengthens health system resilience, foster collaboration across countries, and provides a blueprint for future cooperation in digital health innovation.
Usage of this software tool depends on the quality of its documentation and training material, for WP2-WP4 there will be a tech lead who will liaise between WP1 training activities and local data and research practices. Two key elements in WP1 are the research operations that will simplify, streamline, and automate the collaborations and coordination of activities and products between WPs; and the technical and research capacity building that will provide the learning environment of the required technical skills in the use of modern data engineering tools through formal and informal training sessions, creating and running short workshops, and tutorials that will teach the LatinDiab members how they can use this software tool, and local data engineers on how to implement it.

title: Work package 4. Colombia
text:
WP4 aim to implement a population-based T1D register for surveillance purposes with the aim to estimate the incidence and prevalence of diagnosed T1D and the related complications.
The current Colombian health system remains fragmented, though it is in the process of transitioning towards a universal and equitable coverage model for the entire territory. In this context, we propose to implement a monitoring model based on health records and other health-related data sources from the city of Medellín, a city with approximately 2.63 million residents—and the broader Aburrá Valley, which is home to around 4.25 million inhabitants.
This WP will apply the capture–recapture method to estimate both the incidence and prevalence of type 1 diabetes (T1D) in Medellín and the Aburrá Valley. The approach requires compiling a census of all T1D cases recorded in each data-generating center (e.g., hospitals, outpatient clinics, laboratories, pharmacies), even when the data sources are incomplete. Since each center provides only a fragment of the total cases, the capture–recapture method allows estimation of the total number of cases by combining these partial records. Using statistical models, it becomes possible to generate more accurate estimates of the epidemiological indicators of interest. Figure 3B summarizes the information sources, datasets, variables, and definitions that will be used to build the T1D registry.
The population denominator will be defined based on official projections derived from the 2018 Colombian National Population and Housing Census, adjusted for the study period. Because individuals with T1D are widely dispersed across the health system and there is no centralized or interoperable registry for this condition in Medellín, the Aburrá Valley, or (to our knowledge) anywhere else in Latin America, we will use a non-probabilistic convenience sampling strategy to select data-generating centers. To improve accuracy, cases will be cross-referenced across different sources to identify overlaps. The primary identifier will be the patient’s national identity number, while the secondary identifiers will include first name, last name, and date of birth. For privacy and confidentiality, each individual will then be assigned a unique internal identification code, which will allow anonymization of the databases while facilitating linkage across multiple sources.
Incidence will be calculated as the number of newly diagnosed T1D cases identified during the study period, divided by the total population at risk in Medellín and the Aburrá Valley. Prevalence will be calculated as the total number of existing T1D cases (new and pre-existing) during the same period, divided by the total population of the area. Mortality will be estimated as the number of deaths attributed to T1D during the study period, divided by the total number of individuals with T1D in the population.

title: Work package 3. Mexico
text:
Established in 1943, the Mexican Institute for Social Security (IMSS by its acronym in Spanish) is Mexico’s largest public health institution, responsible for delivering medical care, social security benefits, and pension services to formally employed individuals, retirees, and their dependents such as spouses, children, and in some cases, parents and other dependents, who are legally recognized under IMSS regulations. By the end of 2024, IMSS served a total of 77,430,917 beneficiaries which represent 58.5% of the Mexican population (IMSS, 2025). 
A series of databases from IMSS’ Health Information System comprises a comprehensive set of components and modules that document healthcare delivery processes. The system is designed to generate and disseminate statistical information that supports institutional decision-making. Data includes information on morbidity, mortality, births, population demographics, health services coverage, and the availability of human, infrastructural, material, and financial resources. We will use these datasets to implement the open-source software tool for the set-up of the diabetes register.
The absence of comprehensive registries for T1D impedes outcome monitoring, intervention evaluation, and equitable access to specialized care. Addressing these gaps through targeted research and implementation efforts is essential to reduce long-term complications and improve health outcomes among IMSS patients. Thus, a T1D registry would be a strategic asset for IMSS, enabling real-time tracking of clinical outcomes, identification of high-risk individuals, and monitoring of treatment adherence. By integrating sociodemographic and clinical data, IMSS could design targeted interventions, optimize resource allocation, and assess program effectiveness. Furthermore, the registry could inform policy decisions related to disability benefits, preventive care, and endocrinology service planning, shifting the focus from reactive crisis management to proactive, long-term health strategies and cost containment.

title: Building diabetes registers in Latin America
text:
LatinDiab is a collaborative project that aims to setup modern, scalable, and efficient diabetes registers in Latin America by implementing a well documented set of open-source tools that ensure high quality, reproducible, and transparent research, leading to increase the scientific impact of these resources, improve diabetes care planning and policy making.

The LatinDiab project aims to co-design, implement, and evaluate an open-source software that will be integrated into existing health information systems for the setup of diabetes registers in Mexico, Argentina, and Colombia.
Specifically, it aims to: - Map the available data infrastructure from a data life cycle perspective, map the overall data flows and potential linkage. - Identify the main barriers and facilitators for a successful implementation of the open source software. - Identify and engage key staff involved at any point of the data life cycle to co-design context specific implementation strategies of the open-source software. - Deploy, adapt, and integrate into current health information systems an open-source software to build a diabetes register. - Build research capacity in open science practices and reproducible workflows through online and onsite training sessions. - Co-develop the road map for scaling-up the implementation of the open-source software and the potential implementation of a federated meta-analysis.
Ultimately, the LatinDiab project will provide a proof of concept regarding how continuously updated and automated health data could be linked in order to establish a disease register. Thus, the scope of the open-source software could be transferable to other chronic cardio-metabolic diseases.

title: General description of the WPs
text: The LatinDiab project will be conducted as a multi-country, mixed-methods implementation research project over the course of four years. The project will be implemented in three stages across Argentina, Mexico, and Colombia.

title: General description of the WPs
text:
Stage 1: Data life cycle mapping
The purpose of this stage is to map the available data sources, and identify how the generated data flows in the local health data systems. At this stage it is important to identify potential barriers and enablers for the implementation of the diabetes registers, and key players involved at each step of the data life cycle.

title: General description of the WPs
section: Stage 2: Feasibility study and diabetes algorithm validation
text:
Stage 2: Feasibility study and diabetes algorithm validation
Once we map the local health data life cycle, we will test the deployment of the open-source software in a relative small scale, and the integration in the current local health information systems. We will validate the diabetes register by conducting a pilot study before scaling up the implementation of the software.

title: General description of the WPs
section: Stage 3: Scale up of the diabetes register
text:
Stage 3: Scale up of the diabetes register
Once we have tested the feasibility of the implementation and evaluated the algorithm for identification of people living with diabetes, we will scale up the implementation of the diabetes register.



LatinDiab project stages

title: About us
text:
The LatinDiab project is a collaboration between diabetologists, public health specialists, and epidemiologists from Argentina, Colombia, Mexico, Peru and Denmark.




Argentina








Paula V. Gómez




Paula V. Gómez is physician with a Master’s degree in diabetes and specializations in nutrition and family medicine. She currently works in the research department of the Ministry of Health of La Pampa and coordinates the Integrated Diabetes Practice Unit (UPID) at the Evita Hospital in the province of La Pampa, Argentina.






Colombia








Paula Díaz




Paula Díaz is an Associate Professor at the National School of Public Health at the University of Antioquia, Colombia, and the current president of the Americas Network for Chronic Disease Surveillance.

Denmark








Omar Silverman




Omar Silverman is researcher and Assistant Professor at Aarhus University and the Steno Diabetes Center Aarhus, Denmark.




Daniel Witte is Professor of Diabetes Epidemiology at Aarhus University and at the Steno Diabetes Center Aarhus, Denmark.
